start_cue: A four-to-eight-week single-compound trial with pre-committed exit criteria. Not a stack, not a permanent routine — a test.

weeks.focus: Plan and source before the first dose
weeks.detail: Pick one compound aligned with the single goal you're testing (BPC-157 for injury, CJC/Ipamorelin for mild recovery, topical GHK-Cu for skin). Write down the outcome that would justify continuing past the trial window and the outcome that would end it — before, not during, the trial. Set up tracking for the specific metric you expect to change (recovery time, injury pain at a specific site, skin texture, morning energy).

weeks.focus: Run the trial and track the specific metric
weeks.detail: Single compound, single metric, consistent application. Track the specific variable you pre-committed to, not a vague sense of how you feel. Vague tracking produces vague conclusions; vague conclusions are how people convince themselves a compound is working when it is not.

Read your tracking. Does the data match the continuation criterion you wrote down in week one? If yes, continue or refine. If no, or if the result is ambiguous, stop — ambiguous results that nobody wants to end are the most common failure mode in this category.

graduation: If the continuation criterion was met, the single-compound protocol becomes part of your standing stack. Only then is a second compound worth considering — evaluated the same way, in isolation, with its own exit criteria. Stacking before a single compound has been individually cleared is how the whole system drifts into optimization-theater.
